title: A Giant Buddha Rises Again on the High Line — Made from War Metal!
summary:
Imagine walking on a park above the streets of New York City and suddenly seeing a giant Buddha made of sandstone. That's what's happening on the High Line right now! A 27-foot-tall (that's about as tall as a two-story building) statue called 'The Light That Shines Through the Universe' has been installed at West 30th Street and 10th Avenue.

But this isn't just any Buddha. It's a recreation of the famous Bamiyan Buddhas from Afghanistan. Those ancient statues were carved into a cliffside about 1,500 years ago. They were beloved by people all over the world. But in 2001, the Taliban destroyed them. It was a terrible loss.

Artist Tuan Andrew Nguyen wanted to bring them back — but not as a copy. He calls his statue an "echo" of the originals. And here's the really cool part: the statue's hands are made from melted-down artillery (weapons) that came from Afghanistan! The right hand makes a gesture called Abhaya mudra, which means "fearlessness." The left hand makes the Varada mudra, which means "compassion."

Nguyen says that by remaking the statue, he's keeping the story alive. "You keep a story, idea, or memory alive by retelling," he explained.

The statue looks a bit rough and old-fashioned next to all the shiny glass buildings around it. That's on purpose. The art director of the High Line said it reminds us that time isn't always a straight line — things can come back. The statue will be on view until spring 2027. Go check it out!
why_it_matters: This shows that even when something is destroyed, it can be reborn in a new form, and that art can turn weapons into symbols of peace.

keywords.explanation: A statue of the Buddha, a spiritual teacher, placed on the High Line.
A park built on old railroad tracks above the streets of New York City.
The place in Afghanistan where the original giant Buddha statues were carved into a cliff.
The group that destroyed the original Bamiyan Buddhas in 2001.
What the Taliban did to the ancient Buddha statues – they blew them up.
A symbolic hand gesture; the statue's hands show fearlessness and compassion.

questions.question: Where is the giant Buddha statue installed?
Who destroyed the original Bamiyan Buddhas?
What are the hands of the new statue made from?
What does the Abhaya mudra mean?

background_read: The Bamiyan Buddhas were giant statues carved into a cliff in Afghanistan about 1,500 years ago, and they were a UNESCO World Heritage site.
The Taliban is a group that controlled Afghanistan in the late 1990s and early 2000s and destroyed many historical monuments.
The High Line was once a train track above the streets of New York, and it was turned into a park in 2009.
Article_Structure: WHO: Artist Tuan Andrew Nguyen created the sculpture called 'The Light That Shines Through the Universe'.
WHAT: A 27-foot-tall sandstone Buddha statue with hands made from melted-down weapons from Afghanistan.
WHERE: Installed on the High Line park at West 30th Street and 10th Avenue in New York City.
WHY: To remember the destroyed Bamiyan Buddhas and keep their story alive by 'retelling' it through a new artwork.

perspectives.description: The statue honors lost heritage and promotes peace by using war materials as a symbol of turning violence into art.
Some might feel it's disrespectful to recreate a religious symbol from weapons, or that the statue is too far from the original site.
The artist calls it an 'echo' not a copy, which is an interesting way to think about remembering the past without duplicating it.
